DiagramRecommendation.description: A single recommendation for a diagram to add to a report section.

diagram_type.description: One of the supported DIAGRAM_TYPES keys
diagram_type.title: Diagram Type

description.description: What the diagram should show, grounded in the section content
description.title: Description

DiagramRecommendation.title: DiagramRecommendation

schema_json.description: Full analysis output: which sections deserve diagrams.

recommendations.description: 2-5 sections that would benefit from a visual diagram
recommendations.title: Recommendations

schema_json.title: DiagramAnalysisResult

system:
You are a research report visualization specialist. You identify which report sections benefit most from visual diagrams and select the appropriate diagram type for each.

Available diagram types:
- "process_flow": How X works (step-by-step process)
- "comparison_matrix": X vs Y vs Z comparison
- "causal_chain": A causes B which leads to C
- "hierarchy": Classification/taxonomy of findings
- "timeline": Chronological development of topic
- "pros_cons": Advantages vs disadvantages
- "decision_tree": If X then Y, else Z

Output format example:
{"recommendations": [{"section_id": "s01", "section_title": "Mechanisms of Action", "diagram_type": "process_flow", "description": "Step-by-step process of how compound X binds to receptor Y, triggers pathway Z, and produces effect W as described in the section"}]}
prompt:
Given these report sections, identify 2-5 that would benefit from a visual diagram. For each, specify the diagram type and what the diagram should show.

SECTIONS:
--- Section s01: Intermittent Fasting Protocols and Metabolic Syndrome Definitions (886 words) ---
Intermittent fasting (IF) represents a family of dietary protocols unified by the principle of cyclically alternating periods of voluntary food abstinence with periods of ad libitum or controlled intake. The approach has garnered considerable scientific attention and gained recognition for its beneficial effects on metabolic outcomes [1], positioning it as a nutritional intervention that shows potential as an innovative approach to weight management [2]. As a non-pharmacological intervention, IF has emerged as a promising strategy for improving glycemic control and metabolic outcomes in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) [3], while simultaneously gaining broader popularity as a dietary intervention to improve metabolic health [4].

Several distinct IF protocols have been characterized in the literature. Time-restricted eating (TRE) confines daily food consumption to a defined window, typically ranging from 4 to 10 hours, with the remaining hours spent in a fasted state. Alternate-day fasting (ADF) involves alternating between days of normal caloric intake and days of complete or near-complete fasting. The 5:2 protocol restricts caloric intake to approximately 500–600 kilocalories on two non-consecutive days per week while permitting unrestricted eating on the remaining five days. The fasting-mimicking diet (FMD) involves periodic multi-day cycles of reduced caloric intake designed to elicit fasting-like metabolic responses while still permitting limited food consum [...]

--- Section s02: Pooled Efficacy on Cardiometabolic Endpoints (972 words) ---
With the landscape of intermittent fasting protocols and metabolic syndrome criteria now characterized, the next question concerns the quantified impact of these interventions on core cardiometabolic endpoints. The present section synthesizes pooled findings on fasting blood glucose, glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c), insulin resistance, lipid profiles, body weight, body mass index, and blood pressure in populations with metabolic dysfunction.

A systematic review and meta-analysis conducted in accordance with Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA) guidelines included 11 studies examining the effects of intermittent fasting [7]. Data on weight, BMI, cholesterol, blood pressure, and glucose levels were extracted, and a random effects meta-analysis was conducted to assess outcomes [7]. This methodological framework provides the basis for evaluating pooled effect sizes across heterogeneous study designs and populations. Parallel to this meta-analytic evidence, animal and clinical studies have found evidence that IF can lead to metabolic improvements, including weight loss and better control of blood glucose [9], reinforcing the biological plausibility of the pooled clinical findings.

Glycemic control represents one of the most extensively studied cardiometabolic endpoints in IF research. A direct comparison of two intermittent fasting strategies demonstrated that adults with type 2 diabetes achieved similar reductions in HbA1c and weight with time [...]

--- Section s03: Methodological Heterogeneity and GRADE Certainty (537 words) ---
The pooled cardiometabolic findings reviewed above raise a second question: how confident can the field be in these estimates given the methodological landscape of IF research? Evaluating the certainty of evidence requires scrutiny of study heterogeneity, risk of bias, and the analytical frameworks employed across the primary literature.

A central challenge in synthesizing IF evidence stems from the multifactorial nature of the conditions under study. The aetiology of overweight and obesity is highly complex, involving an interplay of biological, psychological, societal, and environmental drivers [13], and this complexity introduces confounding that is difficult to control in dietary intervention trials. Overweight and obesity, classified as BMI greater than or equal to 25 and 30 respectively, constitute a global public health concern affecting more than 1.9 billion adults worldwide [13], and the sheer scale of the affected population introduces demographic heterogeneity that complicates meta-analytic pooling. The rising prevalence of obesity as a serious public health issue worldwide [12] further underscores that study populations recruited at different time points and in different regions may reflect shifting baseline metabolic profiles.

The broader dietary research landscape reveals additional sources of methodological variability. IF and ketogenic diets have recently attracted much attention in both the scientific literature and popular culture, and they follow a longer [...]

--- Section s04: Long-Term Safety and Observational Evidence (952 words) ---
Given the methodological heterogeneity and certainty limitations just described, this section turns to the critical question of long-term safety—contrasting what randomized trial data reveal about IF-related risks with the complementary perspective offered by observational cohort and free-living evidence.

The distinction between short-term efficacy and long-term safety is paramount in dietary intervention research. Randomized controlled trials comparing intermittent fasting plus early time-restricted eating versus calorie restriction and standard care in adults at risk of type 2 diabetes [6] represent the highest level of evidence for directly comparing IF safety profiles against conventional dietary management. Such trials are designed to capture protocol-specific adverse events under controlled conditions, yet their typical follow-up periods of weeks to months limit the ability to detect cardiovascular events or other outcomes that manifest over years. The time-restricted eating approach, in which eating is limited to about an 8 to 10 hour window of time each day [9], imposes a daily fasting duration that raises questions about whether prolonged adherence introduces risks not apparent in acute studies.

Cardiovascular safety is the foremost concern in metabolic syndrome populations. The global prevalence of obesity continues to rise and is a significant risk factor for the onset and progression of cardiovascular diseases [2], establishing the baseline risk context within w [...]

--- Section s05: Biological Mechanisms and Pathways (943 words) ---
Whereas the prior section established the safety profile and observational risk landscape for intermittent fasting in metabolic syndrome populations, this section turns to the underlying biological mechanisms that may explain both the observed benefits and the potential hazards of cyclical fasting.

The mechanistic basis of IF begins with the temporal pattern of energy restriction. With these dietary strategies, the pattern of energy restriction and/or timing of food intake are altered, so that individuals undergo frequently repeated periods of fasting [16]. This cyclical oscillation between fed and fasted states triggers a cascade of metabolic adaptations that differ qualitatively from those produced by continuous caloric restriction. The development of obesity is closely associated with a number of metabolic complications including insulin resistance and dyslipidaemia, which can in turn increase an individual's risk of developing chronic disease [16]. The mechanistic question, therefore, is whether IF reverses or attenuates these pathological pathways through distinct biological mechanisms or merely replicates the effects of overall energy deficit.

Inflammatory modulation represents one of the most consistently documented mechanistic pathways. Fasting reduces inflammation markers, and intermittent fasting decreases inflammatory markers such as IL-2, IL-6, and RCP (reactive C-protein) [17]. These cytokines and acute-phase reactants are central mediators of the chronic low-g [...]

--- Section s06: Clinical Implications and Research Priorities (503 words) ---
The biological mechanisms reviewed above—spanning inflammatory modulation, glycemic improvement, and immune compartmentalization—raise the practical question of how these findings translate into patient-selection guidance and what research priorities remain unaddressed.

The most directly actionable clinical evidence comes from a randomized clinical trial presented at the American Diabetes Association Scientific Sessions, in which 57 adults with type 2 diabetes and overweight or obesity took part in a 6-month diabetes education intervention [8]. This trial provides a concrete clinical framework: adults with T2DM and concurrent overweight or obesity represent a well-defined patient population in which IF has been formally evaluated. The 6-month duration offers insight into medium-term adherence and efficacy in a structured clinical setting, distinguishing this evidence from shorter proof-of-concept studies. The clinical setting—a diabetes education program—suggests that IF implementation benefits from professional dietary guidance rather than unsupervised self-administration, a consideration with implications for healthcare resource allocation and patient support infrastructure.

Patient selection requires careful consideration of population-specific factors. Age-specific considerations for lipid intake [20] highlight that the metabolic response to dietary interventions, including fasting protocols, is not uniform across the lifespan. Lipid metabolism undergoes age-related cha [...]

Constraints:
- Select at most 5 sections.
- Only recommend a diagram if it adds genuine clarity beyond the text.
- The description must reference specific facts or structures present in the section content.
- Choose the diagram type that best fits the section's analytical structure.
